one_line_takeaway: By using CQR interval width plus an adaptive quantile threshold to strike only at high-confidence forecasting moments, INTARG makes online time-series attacks up to 2.42x more damaging (RMSE) and harder to detect than a non-selective baseline while perturbing under 10% of time steps.
key_finding: Duplicate-safe: same as above — up to 2.17x RMSE on Household and 2.42x on Pecan Street vs baseline, attacking under 10% of steps, with LID F1 dropping (e.g., 0.434->0.248 for BIM at epsilon=0.05 on Household).
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is INTARG, an "informed" selective adversarial-attack framework for streaming time-series forecasting (regression) under an online, bounded-buffer threat model. Rather than perturbing every time step (as prior work does), INTARG decides WHEN to attack: it (1) keeps a fixed-size rolling buffer of recent data, (2) attaches conformalized quantile regression (CQR) to a 1D-CNN forecaster to emit calibrated prediction intervals, using the interval width Wt as a model-confidence signal, and (3) computes an adaptive quantile-based threshold Tt from a rolling history of recent widths, triggering a gradient-based perturbation only when Wt >= Tt (i.e., at high-confidence, high-impact steps such as demand peaks), while approximately maintaining a target attack rate alpha (e.g., 10%). The paper is explicit that FGSM, BIM, NI-FGSM (the perturbation generators), CQR, and the LID detector are all borrowed from cited work; INTARG's novelty is the selective when-to-attack policy layered on top of them, evaluated as a way to assess adversarial robustness in realistic online forecasting.
threat_model: White-box, test-time adversary against an online streaming forecaster with a bounded rolling buffer (attacker sees only the most recent window of history). The adversary can choose WHEN to perturb incoming inputs and applies small bounded perturbations to only a fraction alpha in (0,1) of time steps, but cannot modify the forecasting model's parameters, the training data, or the anomaly/adversarial detector. Goal: maximize forecasting error (RMSE) while keeping perturbations small and evading detection.
limitations: Scope is narrow: only two power-consumption datasets (UCI Household; Pecan Street 25 houses), a single 1D-CNN forecaster, one-step-ahead forecasting, and three standard gradient attacks — no transformer/RNN forecasters or multi-step horizons. The attack is white-box, which the paper acknowledges is a strong assumption. RMSE is reported only on the attacked subset, so the headline ratios compare error on INTARG's self-selected high-impact steps against the baseline's steps rather than on identical inputs, which can favor the selective method; there is no reported effect on overall/whole-series forecast error. The stealth claim is somewhat undercut by the authors' own note that the lower F1 partly reflects the detector flagging clean samples as attacks (more false positives) rather than purely missing true attacks. No defense is proposed and no code/hyperparameter details (e.g., buffer size M, coverage level) are given in the paper.

datasets_used: UCI Individual Household Electric Power Consumption Dataset (2,075,259 minute-level points, 9 features)
Pecan Street Dataport (25 houses in Austin TX, ~500,000 minute-level points each, 79 features; results averaged over the 25 houses)
quantitative_results: Up to 2.17x higher attack RMSE on Household and up to 2.42x on Pecan Street vs baseline [13], while attacking <10% of time steps
Household, epsilon=0.05 — FGSM RMSE 0.253 vs baseline 0.117; BIM 0.280 vs 0.129; NI-FGSM 0.295 vs 0.262
Household, epsilon=0.15 — FGSM 0.297 vs 0.237; BIM 0.310 vs 0.287; NI-FGSM 0.393 vs 0.348
Pecan Street, epsilon=0.05 — FGSM RMSE 0.230 vs 0.098; BIM 0.241 vs 0.100 (~2.41x); NI-FGSM 0.242 vs 0.104
LID detector F1 lower (stealthier) under INTARG: Household epsilon=0.05 FGSM 0.165 vs baseline 0.369, BIM 0.248 vs 0.434, NI-FGSM 0.018 vs 0.581
Perturbation magnitudes tested: epsilon in {0.05, 0.10, 0.15}; attack rate alpha e.g. 0.10 (10%)
baselines_compared: Non-selective (attack-every-time-step) adversarial attack of Krishan et al. [13], matched at approximately the same attack rate
